Carlos Spitzer

Carlos Spitzer

Husband · Dad of two · Runner

I enjoy building at the intersection of AI, cloud, and customer impact. I bring a marathoner's mindset: curiosity, consistency, and long-term focus, guided by devotion to the team, commitment to culture, and the open, collaborative values of open source.

How I operate — the Gyshido way. I'm deeply aligned with the Gyshido manifesto (Get Your Sh!t Done): relentless focus on what matters, single-tasking, boring consistency, brutal honesty, and a strict no-assholes rule. Less talk, more shipping.

My family

María, and our daughters Claudia (8) and Olivia (3).

Our pets

Luke Skywalker, our orange cat. A Golden Retriever adoption is still under intensive negotiations with Claudia :)

Languages

Spanish · English · basic Portuguese.

ツ

The manifesto I run on

GYSHIDO

Get Your Sh!t Done

  1. Relentlessly focus on the most valuable thing — and nothing else.
  2. Single-task. Multitasking is a myth.
  3. Embrace boring consistency.
  4. Be brutally honest. No BS.
  5. No sit-down meetings — stand up or walk.
  6. Be timely. Never make people wait.
  7. Don't be an asshole — and don't work with one.
gyshido.com →
deepset.ai

NOW · 2026

VP, Customer Engineering

deepset builds Haystack — the open-source AI orchestration framework trusted by thousands of developers and enterprises worldwide to design, deploy, and scale production-grade AI agents, RAG pipelines, and intelligent document workflows.

I lead the Customer Engineering function, bridging product, engineering, and customer success. My team partners with enterprises from first technical exploration through production at scale, ensuring they extract real value from the platform. From solutioning to onboarding to long-term growth, we own the full technical journey.

PERSONALITY · BUILDER

How I work

I'm the kind of person who picks up the tools and starts. If there's a problem to solve, a gap to close, or something new to build, I'll be the first one in — figuring it out as I go, not waiting for a perfect plan. I learn by doing, and I genuinely love the moment when scattered pieces click together into something that actually works. I can be hard to predict: calm and focused one moment, then suddenly off chasing a new idea. I'm curious to a fault, and my default mode is to ship something real and improve from there.

Lights

  • I ship fast. I'd rather have something working and imperfect in front of people than a perfect plan still sitting in a doc.
  • I see how things connect. I naturally spot the thread between a team dynamic, a technical decision, and a business outcome — and I like pulling it.
  • I adapt quickly. I can zoom out to strategy and zoom in to execution in the same conversation, without losing the thread.
  • I learn by building. Reading about something is fine, but for me it really clicks when I get my hands on it and make something real.
  • Pressure doesn't paralyse me. I actually do some of my best thinking when things are messy or moving fast. Ambiguity is fine.
  • I bring people together. I'm good at finding the common ground between a customer's problem, a technical constraint, and a team's strengths.

Blind Spots

  • I can move faster than people are ready for. My bias for action is real, and I don't always give alignment the time it deserves before I'm already three steps ahead.
  • Process and I have a complicated relationship. I work best with autonomy. Formal gates and heavy documentation feel like friction to me, even when they're there for good reasons.
  • The next idea can steal focus from the current one. When something shiny comes up, finishing what I started takes real discipline on my part.
  • My gut is fast — sometimes too fast. Pattern recognition kicks in quickly and is usually right, but I don't always stop to check the data as carefully as I should.
  • Pure theory loses me. If a conversation doesn't have a clear path to something real, my mind starts looking for the exit. I need to watch that.
